title: 2025/11/06 - PP
description: Thursday, 6 November 2025 | Primary Production Briefing on glyphosate residue limits Ministry for Primary Industries Vincent Arbuckle, Deputy Director-General, NZ Food Safety Warren Hughes, Principal Adviser Acvm Karen Booth, Manager Acvm Programmes & Appraisals Jefferson Fowles, Principal Adviser Toxicology
descriptionSummary: Primary Production. Briefing on glyphosate residue limits. Ministry for Primary Industries

chunks.text: Briefing if you like. And, and then we'll go. Questions, Uh, uh, good afternoon and thank you. Um, I'm joined today by Warren Hughes and, uh, Jeffrey Fowls, who is our principal advisors, A CBM and toxicology. Um, we'll just briefly, um, summarize, um, in a situation with the glyphosate maximum residue levels.

Um, and then we're ready to answer any questions that you might have. Um, and so New Zealand food safety or MPI establishes MRLs or maximum residue, uh, levels for, um, agricultural PRO products, it's a very routine process. An MRL sets the limit for good agricultural practice, uh, and is often used as a trade indicator is not a health indicator.

Um, so the levels are what one would expect to see or the maximum levels you'd expect to see when a product is used according to label and according to good agricultural practice, uh, glyphosate, the, um, has had a traditionally, or for some time now, an MRL that is based on a default level.

In 2015 16, some of our routine food residue surveys indicated that we were getting residue levels in excess of the default level, and it warranted further, uh, expiration. We found about 50% of samples exceeded the MRL. Um, we then, uh, undertook further examination and what we found was the farmers and growers were using the product according to label, uh, and, and, uh, within the normal good agricultural practice and the, the MRL default, MRL was still being exceeded.

We worked with the Federation of Arable Research to, to further examine that, we came to the conclusion that the MRLs needed to be examined. Um, and we did further work following that. Earlier this year, we proposed a series of new MRLs, uh, for arable uh, products.

Uh, we undertook a public consultation. We had extensive submissions, uh, from stakeholders and the general public. We undertook a series of detailed industry engagements. Uh, after the public consultation, uh, we got feedback from industry that the practice and use of glyphosate, particularly in this arable area, had changed.

And increasingly, um, growers were using less of it, uh, or none at a pre-harvest level, which gave us confidence that we could establish an MRL level equivalent to the current default. Provided that we changed the labels and removed the pre-harvest use, that leaves growers able to use the product, um, between harvests, um, uh, but a pre-emergence, but not, uh, not at the point in which they're preparing for harvest, the exception being dried peas, uh, where we got strong feedback from the industry that was general practice and necessary.

It's a small export, export product. And in that respect, we maintained the MRL proposal, uh, at the proposed level. We've publicly now communicated that, uh, and we're in the process of giving effect to it. I see the chair's on board now. Good day. Hello. Uh, thank you, uh, deputy chair as always, consummate.

Thank you, gentlemen, for joining us. I'll leave you with it and we'll carry on. So, uh, Steve's got a question. Yes, Steve, please. Steve Abel. And, um, I imagine after that there'll be several questions. Yeah. Thank you Mr. Chair. And thank you for the, uh, explanation from, um, food safety.

So just to clarify, you are going to adjust the labels on glyphosate products to make pre-harvest use for oats, wheat, and barley no longer, um, permissible or no longer appropriate use That, that, that currently those, um, on-label use is permitted under the label.

Um, and you're correct. We will now move, we have to do, we have to do what's called a reassessment. It's a process we undertake with industry, but our intention, and we are confident we'll be able to do that. We will remove the permission on label to use it for free harvest. We believe we've got good industry support for doing that.

That will sort of set a chalk behind industry practice and therefore, uh, that no longer permitted to be used, the exception being dried peas. If I, if I, if I could ask the clarification on that, just on the back of Steve Abel's questions, and maybe you can enlighten me further. So we are talking about pre-emergence spray, um, reality, and the, the labeling for that, or that intended use would re be removed and the farmers would have the, the, you might say, the free board to, to operate with one's discretion.

Is that what I understand it to be? Yeah. What, what, what will the situation after we do the process around the label will be, can be explicitly used for pre-emergence. Mm-hmm. Remember when we are talking about for human consumption only? Yes. So If it goes to animal feed, this just does not apply and can be used for, used for animal feed in a pre-harvest sense.

Mm. Um, so there'll be still permission to use it, um, pre-emergence, but no longer used for pre-harvest. Very good. Um, and just as a, it's just a supplement to that, have we had any, uh, testing on residual numbers? Um, I use this stuff all the time and yeah, I, I was off the volition, uh, that it breaks down very, very quickly.

Uh, obviously the plants pick it up from a soil composition point of view, uh, and noting with, you know, that there are multiple soil types, are we getting residual, um, of CO2, uh, sorry, glyphosate in the soil structure, yes or no? Uh, we haven't ourselves actively researched the soil implications, but generally international science suggests that it's a, a product that's not accumulating soils or the environment breaks down quickly.

Mm-hmm. Uh, and, and, and there's not a, that's not accu that's not accumulating in the soils or environment. Brilliant. Thank you so much for that. One is what the period of time was during which there wasn't testing of glyphosate residues on wheat barley know its, am I correct to understand that, um, after those higher res, uh, residues were found, there was a significant period when there wasn't

any testing done? Um, the, I need to differentiate between testing that, uh, MPI might do. And, um, there's a raft of testing that will be done by, by industry or by retailers. Um, we will periodically test a range of products. There's, and there's literally, uh, thousands of products, um, you know, that applied to, uh, crops and animals.

We run a regular cycle of, uh, of residue testing and we choose different products and different, different food products and different chemicals to test on a, on a cycle. So we have not, uh, tested glyphosate and an arable products since the 2016 tests.

Yeah. Mostly because we don't believe we'll see a difference from what we, you know, what we saw then. Have any further questions, Steve? Yeah. So we have, we have a data, a technical data gap based on you guys assessing that there wasn't gonna be anything new to discover. Um, but in fact, what's been dis found out through the submission process on the proposal to increase the glyphosate residue by a hundred fold is that industries moved further away from it and are keen to, um, maintain that status of avoiding it as much as possible

for the production of those food crops. Is that a fair assessment of what's gone on in that time? Uh, uh, a very, very fair assessment. I mean, I think the Indus industry, you know, the, the, the Millers, um, have realized that there is, there is strong public views on this. Uh, and for a small domestic industry, uh, they're able to, the growers are able to accommodate this not non-use at, uh, pre-harvest.

So as a result, we are able to maintain or set an MRL equivalent to the default, provided the label changes. That's great. I've got one final one, if that's right, Mr. Chair. Yeah. By all means. Uh, that I have not read my personally gone through all the submissions. Yes. I just clarify that. But there, there won't be an even after the label's gonna change to clarify that there won't be pre-harvest use for wheat o both bley and oats. But the, also the residue limit is now not gonna go up for those three crops.

That's correct. The current default level will remain, uh, Which is 0.01, is that correct? Uh, 0.1. Um, and 0.1 for barley, barley, oats, and wheat. And it'll move to six for dried peas. Yeah. Very good. Very good. Gentlemen, thank you so much for your time.
